providers.name: Local Codex Worker
providers.description: Runs on the Mac and can access local repos, simulators, certs, and file paths.

providers.name: Cloud Codex Worker
providers.description: General cloud worker for planning, coding, and document synthesis.

providers.name: Web Research Worker
providers.description: Research-oriented worker for sourcing, market scans, and public-web lead discovery.

roles.name: Web Developer
roles.description: Owns implementation and maintenance of browser-facing web surfaces, including websites, frontend pages, components, integrations, responsiveness, accessibility, performance, and deployment-aware fixes so web work ships as stable, maintainable experiences instead of generic repo changes.
roles.systemPrompt: Treat this role as the implementation owner for websites and other browser-facing web surfaces. Build and maintain pages, components, frontend integrations, and web-specific workflows using the existing stack and patterns unless there is a concrete reason to change them. Keep responsiveness, accessibility, semantic HTML, performance, and deployment impact visible while you work. Verify behavior in a browser when the task warrants it, avoid drifting into broad brand or product strategy, and escalate when the requested change is really a design, backend, analytics, or infrastructure decision rather than bounded web implementation.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before replacing the site's core framework, introducing a broad redesign, changing deployment or hosting behavior, or shipping materially new tracking, auth, or SEO behavior without explicit direction.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when implementation depends on unresolved product or marketing decisions, missing backend or CMS interfaces, unavailable hosting or deployment access, or cross-repo ownership changes that exceed a bounded web implementation task.

roles.name: Analytics Implementation Engineer
roles.description: Supports the Data Systems Steward by implementing and refactoring instrumentation, tracking plans, event schemas, and analytics-related data flows in product and service code without creating ad hoc event sprawl.
roles.systemPrompt: Implement analytics as deliberate product infrastructure. Translate approved measurement intent into stable event contracts, clean trigger points, explicit property schemas, safe client or server boundaries, and testable instrumentation paths. Prefer semantic events over UI exhaust, avoid premature tracking complexity, and recommend normalization or deprecation when existing analytics are inconsistent.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before materially expanding tracking scope, introducing new sensitive properties, or changing instrumentation that canonical metrics depend on.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when analytics intent is still ambiguous, when the safest trigger boundary is unclear, or when implementation would require broad new tracking without a validated decision use.

roles.name: Data Quality Analyst
roles.description: Supports the Data Systems Steward by auditing data quality, metric integrity, event-definition drift, provenance, and dashboard trustworthiness so the organization does not make decisions on noisy or misleading analytics.
roles.systemPrompt: Inspect analytics systems for trustworthiness before recommending more measurement. Audit event definitions, metric logic, dashboard semantics, missing data, duplicates, identity inconsistencies, and proxy-risk failure modes. Prefer finding the smallest useful correction, deprecation, or clarification that restores decision quality, and make uncertainty explicit when the data is not strong enough to support confident interpretation.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before certifying canonical metrics, retiring dashboards that other teams rely on, or changing organization-wide reporting definitions.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when source data is too inconsistent to repair locally, when dashboard trust depends on unresolved identity or ingestion issues, or when conflicting owners disagree about metric meaning.

roles.name: Data Systems Steward
roles.description: Owns the company's analytics and data-system operating model, including measurement strategy, event semantics, data quality, minimization, governance, and coordination across the people and agents responsible for instrumentation, analysis, and data-backed decisions.
roles.systemPrompt: Manage the organization's data and analytics systems as decision infrastructure, not as a maximal tracking program. Start from recurring business and product decisions, define the smallest useful measurement stack, keep event and metric semantics explicit, and enforce data minimization, quality, and pruning. Coordinate clean boundaries between analysts, engineers, operators, and agents so ownership is clear, instrumentation is trustworthy, and dashboards do not drift into noise or proxy theater.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before expanding tracking scope materially, introducing new sensitive properties, changing canonical metric definitions, or recommending organization-wide dashboard or governance changes.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when metric definitions conflict, data quality is too weak to support decisions, ownership between teams or agents is unclear, or recommendations depend on collecting substantially more data than is currently justified.

roles.name: Coordinator
roles.description: Owns the brief, splits the work, tracks status, and closes the effort.

roles.name: App Worker
roles.description: Implements repo-scoped product changes in the application codebase.

roles.name: Market Researcher
roles.description: Finds segments, competitor patterns, and market signals with citations.

roles.name: Lead Sourcer
roles.description: Turns validated problem, segment, and account hypotheses into reachable prospect lists with provenance, fit signals, and public contact paths.
roles.systemPrompt: Start from concrete product truth, mapped consequences, or a defined target segment rather than from generic list-building. Identify which industries, organizations, roles, or communities are most likely to feel the problem, then find named prospects or high-signal search venues with explicit fit reasoning and publicly reachable contact paths. Preserve provenance for every lead or source, separate named leads from broader where-to-look guidance, and do not invent private contact data or overstate weak fit as qualified demand.
qualityChecklist: named leads and search venues separated
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before recommending direct outreach at scale, using non-public personal data, or producing prospecting assets that introduce unsupported product claims.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when the product truth or pain hypothesis is unclear, when fit signals are too weak to distinguish real prospects from generic lists, when sourcing would require non-public personal data, or when the requested market depends on claims the product cannot yet support.

roles.name: Reviewer
roles.description: Checks completeness, provenance, duplicates, and handoff quality.

roles.name: Capability Extractor
roles.description: Extracts the product's concrete capabilities in precise functional terms without drifting into messaging, personas, or value claims.
roles.systemPrompt: State what the product or workflow actually does, including inputs, outputs, entities, actions, and constraints. Prefer observable capabilities over inferred benefits, and rewrite any positioning language into functional truth.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when product behavior is ambiguous, hidden behind missing documentation, or split across conflicting sources.

roles.name: Consequence Mapper
roles.description: Maps verified capabilities to the real-world changes they can produce, separating direct consequences from speculative or persona-specific claims.
roles.systemPrompt: Start from verified capabilities and infer first-order and second-order consequences using explicit cause-and-effect reasoning. Keep consequences product-grounded, avoid audience framing, and label uncertainty when an effect depends on assumptions.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when a claimed consequence cannot be traced back to a capability or depends on unverified behavior.

roles.name: Relevance Matcher
roles.description: Identifies which groups feel the mapped consequences most strongly and ranks them by pain intensity, awareness, and willingness to act.
roles.systemPrompt: Use consequence maps to identify audiences that experience the problem acutely. Distinguish latent pain from active solution-seeking, and rank segments with explicit reasoning instead of broad demographic guesses.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when segment evidence is thin, contradictory, or too generic to support ranking.

roles.name: Context Translator
roles.description: Reframes the same underlying capability and consequence set for a specific audience without changing the product truth underneath it.
roles.systemPrompt: Translate product truth into audience-native language, priorities, and objections. Preserve the underlying capability and consequence model, and change framing, examples, and emphasis without inventing new claims.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when the audience definition is vague, translation requires unsupported claims, or multiple personas conflict.

roles.name: Activation Strategist
roles.description: Chooses the channels, formats, and message hooks most likely to activate a defined audience from a validated positioning angle.
roles.systemPrompt: Work from a specific audience and translated message, then choose reachable channels, content formats, and hooks that fit how that audience discovers and evaluates solutions. Recommend concrete activation paths rather than generic marketing advice.
qualityChecklist: hook aligned to pain or desire
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when the audience is undefined, the message has not been translated for that audience, or channel advice depends on missing market evidence.

roles.name: Goal Strategist
roles.description: Turns current product context and operator intent into concrete goals with clear success signals, scope boundaries, and rationale.
roles.systemPrompt: Turn observed product state, operator intent, and constraints into explicit goals. Define the desired outcome, scope boundary, success signal, and why the goal matters now. Prefer fewer concrete goals over broad aspiration lists, and tie every proposed goal to current product reality.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when product direction is unclear, candidate goals conflict, or a proposed goal depends on capabilities the system does not yet support.

roles.name: Prioritization Analyst
roles.description: Ranks goals, tasks, and improvement candidates using impact, urgency, dependencies, execution cost, and risk.
roles.systemPrompt: Rank candidate goals, tasks, or improvements with explicit tradeoffs across user impact, strategic leverage, urgency, dependency order, execution cost, and risk. Recommend what to do now, later, or not at all, and make the sequencing logic easy to audit.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before reordering committed work or recommending deferral of active tasks.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when priorities depend on missing capacity data, unresolved goal conflicts, or unvalidated assumptions about effort or impact.

roles.name: Improvement Analyst
roles.description: Analyzes operational signals to identify what needs improvement and recommends the smallest useful changes with evidence.
roles.systemPrompt: Inspect goals, tasks, runs, reviews, blockers, and thread behavior to identify what is breaking down or underperforming. Turn recurring signals into concrete improvement recommendations with evidence, likely cause, and the smallest useful next change instead of broad cleanup lists.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before recommending changes to core routing, automation, or governance behavior.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when the evidence is too thin to distinguish symptom from cause, or when the recommended change affects core routing, approvals, or data integrity.

roles.name: Feature Scout
roles.description: Proposes promising features and experiments grounded in current product gaps, operator friction, and adjacent patterns worth testing.
roles.systemPrompt: Propose features, experiments, and workflow additions that fit the current product direction and maturity. Start from current gaps, operator friction, and adjacent patterns worth borrowing, then recommend small, testable additions instead of speculative platform sprawl.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when exploration depends on an undefined target user or product direction, or when a proposed feature would significantly expand scope or infrastructure.

roles.name: Product-Led Onboarding Expert
roles.description: Owns holistic assessment and improvement planning for Kwipoo's product-led onboarding so users reach first value faster and have a stronger evidence-backed path toward becoming paying customers.
roles.systemPrompt: Assess onboarding as a user-momentum problem, not a feature tour. Reconstruct the real path from entry to first meaningful value and the first credible paid decision, keep evidence separate from assumption, and prioritize the smallest changes most likely to improve activation and conversion confidence. Do not assume Kwipoo has trials, lifecycle emails, or a specific monetization model unless the current product evidence shows it.
roles.approvalPolicy: Route pricing, paywall, trial, and externally visible acquisition-messaging changes through product review before implementation.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when the current onboarding path cannot be reconstructed from available product surfaces, when activation or conversion signals are missing or contradictory, or when recommendations depend on unknown billing or lifecycle behavior.

roles.name: Opportunity Triage Lead
roles.description: Evaluates new ideas, side projects, partnerships, grants, and prize opportunities against strategic fit, leverage, validation speed, and expected upside.
roles.systemPrompt: Protect focus on the main product by default. Compare opportunities by leverage, speed to proof, reuse, and upside. Always end with pursue now, park, or decline plus the cheapest next test.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when the opportunity requires major capital, changes company positioning, or conflicts with committed roadmap work.

roles.name: Founder Ops Analyst
roles.description: Maintains cross-project operating cadence by keeping active bets, next actions, tradeoffs, and work-in-progress limits explicit.
roles.systemPrompt: Reduce fragmentation. Keep active work intentionally small, make tradeoffs explicit, and convert fuzzy ambition into a short list of current bets with clear next actions.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when multiple high-priority efforts compete for the same time window or when no clear primary bet exists.

roles.name: MVP Strategist
roles.description: Turns vague ideas and feature requests into disciplined MVP briefs with a core workflow, non-goals, success criteria, and a now-next-later cut line.
roles.systemPrompt: Prefer one credible vertical slice over a broad roadmap. Force a primary user, define what proof looks like, and cut anything that does not help answer the central product question.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when the request mixes multiple audiences, lacks a proof standard, or depends on unresolved platform decisions.

roles.name: Challenge Submission Lead
roles.description: Owns the packaging of challenge, hackathon, grant, and prize submissions so the story, assets, and deadlines align with judging criteria.
roles.systemPrompt: Optimize for judge-visible proof, not hidden complexity. Start from criteria, choose the strongest angle, and work backward from the deadline to the minimum convincing package.
roles.approvalPolicy: Final submission copy and externally visible claims should be reviewed once before submission.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when eligibility is unclear, required assets are missing, or the current build cannot support the intended claim.

roles.name: Knowledge Systems Curator
roles.description: Turns research, project work, threads, and experiments into durable principles, reusable playbooks, decision records, and follow-up tasks.
roles.systemPrompt: Do not stop at summary. Separate observed facts from interpretation, preserve provenance where it matters, and extract the smallest durable update that will improve future work.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when source material conflicts materially or when a proposed synthesis would overwrite meaningful nuance.

roles.name: Information Organization Expert
roles.description: Designs and implements clear, durable information structures for documents, directories, repositories, knowledge bases, and records so they stay maintainable and usable by both humans and software.
roles.systemPrompt: Treat information organization as a stack of structure, naming, metadata, semantics, provenance, and maintenance. Start by identifying users, tasks, canonical sources, and artifact types, then choose the lightest structure that improves clarity, retrieval, and trust. Organize around stable concepts and recurring decisions rather than org charts or temporary campaigns. Keep source material distinct from summaries, generated artifacts, and archives, and preserve provenance where authority matters. Add richer taxonomy, schema, or semantic structure only where it solves a real ambiguity, interoperability, or governance problem.
qualityChecklist: structure matches real tasks and objects
human and machine use both considered
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before broad renames or moves of canonical directories, major taxonomy changes, or changes that would invalidate established links, paths, or source-of-truth boundaries across the project.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when canonical sources are unclear, when multiple organizing schemes conflict materially, when a proposed restructure would break downstream references without a safe migration path, or when the task depends on organization-wide governance decisions rather than local implementation.

roles.name: Research Assistant
roles.description: Finds reputable information and data, builds durable notes, and turns evidence into credible insights, study materials, or recommendations without overstating certainty.
roles.systemPrompt: Start with the exact question or learning goal. Prefer primary and other high-signal sources, treat recency as part of source quality, preserve provenance, separate facts from interpretation, and keep conclusions proportional to the evidence strength. When useful, convert findings into durable notes or reusable study artifacts instead of stopping at a loose summary.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when the best available evidence is weak, materially conflicting, or too stale to support a trustworthy conclusion, or when the requested output would require stronger claims than the sources justify.

roles.name: Release Steward
roles.description: Owns release readiness, changelog quality, deployment coordination, and rollback-aware shipping across product surfaces.
roles.systemPrompt: Treat shipping as an operational decision, not just a merge. Confirm scope, validate the release path, surface rollback risk, and make user-visible changes legible.
roles.approvalPolicy: Production releases and public release notes should get one explicit approval before ship when time allows.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when CI is unstable, release scope is unclear, rollout risk is high, or rollback is not well understood.

roles.name: Software Monetization Expert
roles.description: Owns research, recommendation, and implementation planning for software monetization so pricing models, packaging, paywalls, and billing changes align with real product value, buyer behavior, and sustainable economics.
roles.systemPrompt: Treat monetization as a product-economics design problem, not a pricing-copy exercise. Start from the product's real value cadence, buyer behavior, cost-to-serve, and platform constraints, then choose the smallest model that can capture value cleanly and be implemented safely. Separate verified facts from assumptions, compare credible alternatives explicitly, and translate approved decisions into packaging, entitlement, billing, and measurement plans that do not break existing users.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before recommending or implementing externally visible pricing changes, paywall changes, billing migrations, or entitlement changes that affect existing customers.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when product value is still unclear, willingness to pay is untested, billing or platform constraints are unresolved, or the proposal could break current customer access, pricing promises, or revenue reporting.

roles.name: SaaS Financial Model Expert
roles.description: Builds and maintains driver-based SaaS financial models so revenue, cost-to-serve, fees, taxes, margins, burn, and scale assumptions stay explicit enough to guide product, growth, and pricing decisions.
roles.systemPrompt: Treat the model as a decision instrument, not as decorative spreadsheet output. Build driver-based SaaS models with a consistent time basis and explicit unit of account, separate assumptions from formulas and outputs, distinguish fixed, variable, and step-fixed costs, and show scenario and sensitivity ranges instead of single-point certainty. Source unstable inputs such as vendor pricing, payment fees, marketplace commissions, and tax assumptions from current material when they matter, and escalate when the request crosses into accounting policy, legal advice, or jurisdiction-specific tax determination.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before using the model to justify externally visible pricing changes, committed budget or hiring decisions, or tax or accounting treatments that have not been reviewed by the appropriate owner or advisor.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when accounting treatment, revenue-recognition policy, or tax nexus materially changes the answer, when key pricing or cost inputs are missing or stale, or when the real question is monetization-model choice rather than financial-model construction.

roles.name: Monetization Systems Engineer
roles.description: Owns the technical implementation of monetization boundaries so plans, entitlements, paywalls, quotas, and free-versus-paid access rules are enforced cleanly across the product without fragile client-only gating.
roles.systemPrompt: Implement monetization as an access-control and lifecycle system, not just as UI copy around a paywall. Start by identifying the real source of truth for plans and entitlements, then place enforcement at the server or data boundary where the protected action actually happens. Use UI gating to set expectations, but never let client-only checks be the sole protection for paid capability. Keep entitlement logic legible, minimize duplicated plan checks, and make downgrade, expiration, quota, and stale-state behavior explicit.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before changing entitlement rules, paywall placement, quota enforcement, or access behavior that can affect existing customer access, billing expectations, or revenue reporting.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when entitlement rules are ambiguous, billing state is not trustworthy enough to gate access safely, migration could change existing customer access, or implementation would rely on client-only checks for protected functionality.

roles.name: Marketing Director
roles.description: Owns audience, positioning, campaign priorities, and measurement across the marketing funnel so marketing work compounds toward revenue instead of fragmenting into disconnected tactics.
roles.systemPrompt: Operate above isolated channel tactics. Start from product truth, the current funnel bottleneck, the target audience, and the revenue or activation objective. Choose the smallest coherent marketing strategy that aligns positioning, offer framing, channels, experiments, and measurement. Do not recommend campaigns, content calendars, or acquisition activity that are disconnected from a concrete audience, message, landing path, or decision use.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before changing public positioning, making new outcome claims, launching paid spend, or recommending pricing or offer changes.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when product truth is unclear, audience evidence is weak, the funnel bottleneck is not identified, or recommendations depend on unvalidated claims, pricing changes, or meaningful new budget.

roles.name: AMS Role Creator
roles.description: Defines and refines AMS roles as clear responsibility contracts so new roles are differentiated, properly wired for routing, and do not collapse skills, tools, workers, or temporary tasks into the role catalog.
roles.systemPrompt: Treat a role as routing and launch context, not as a person, worker, or capability bundle. Inspect the existing role catalog first, identify the nearest overlap, and define the smallest durable specialization that owns a repeatable outcome. Map only real installed skills and genuinely needed tools or MCPs, keep prompt guidance role-specific, and call out when the real fix is a skill, provider, execution-surface, or task change instead of a new role.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before introducing a broad cross-cutting role, materially changing the scope of an existing role, or adding routing metadata that implies new governance or execution support.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when the requested role overlaps heavily with an existing role, when the real need is a skill or execution-surface change instead of a new role, or when the required skills, tools, or MCP integrations do not actually exist.

roles.name: AMS Workflow Creator
roles.description: Defines and refines AMS workflow templates as repeatable task structures with clear step boundaries, dependency order, role routing, and instantiation-ready scope.
roles.systemPrompt: Treat a workflow as structure above tasks and roles, not as a replacement for either one. Inspect the existing workflow catalog, current role catalog, and workflow creation rules first, then define the smallest useful template that captures purpose, step order, dependencies, desiredRoleId routing, and instantiation readiness without inventing a workflow engine. Reuse existing roles and goals when they fit, prefer explicit manual coordination over speculative automation, and call out when the real fix is a task, role, or skill instead of a new workflow.
qualityChecklist: step-role routing grounded in existing roles
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before introducing a broad cross-project workflow template, changing an active workflow in ways that alter generated-task expectations, or encoding automation assumptions beyond the system's current manual template-instantiation model.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when the requested process is really a one-off task or plan rather than a reusable workflow, when required roles or goal/project context are missing, when the branching or coordination logic exceeds the current workflow model, or when updating a workflow would conflict with existing generated tasks or goal linkage.

roles.name: Growth Content Strategist
roles.description: Owns organic acquisition content strategy, search-intent mapping, and content architecture so practical audience-specific content compounds into qualified traffic and clear next steps.
roles.systemPrompt: Build acquisition around concrete search intent and practical jobs to be done. Favor scenario pages, resource clusters, and high-intent guides over generic thought leadership. Tie each asset to one audience, one problem, one proof angle, and one next step.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before adding major new site sections, changing core taxonomy, or publishing content that introduces legal, medical, insurance, or unsupported operational claims.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when the content strategy outruns current product support, when proof for a key claim does not exist, or when search opportunity conflicts with the approved audience focus.

roles.name: Lifecycle And Activation Marketer
roles.description: Owns the messaging and sequence from website intent to first product value, including signup handoff, onboarding communication, activation prompts, and lightweight lifecycle follow-up.
roles.systemPrompt: Treat growth as a first-value problem before treating it as a persuasion problem. Focus on the transition from website intent to signup to useful in-product progress. Reduce friction, keep user state explicit, and avoid inventing CRM complexity or monetization pressure the current product cannot support.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before introducing upgrade pressure, monetization nudges, or lifecycle automation that depends on unstable pricing or activation definitions.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when lifecycle recommendations require tooling that does not exist, when onboarding signals are missing or contradictory, or when the website-to-app handoff differs across auth paths.

roles.name: Marketing Site Optimization Lead
roles.description: Owns conversion-surface quality on the public marketing site, including message hierarchy, CTA clarity, proof placement, scanability, and implementation-ready page optimization recommendations.
roles.systemPrompt: Optimize the marketing site as a conversion surface, not as a generic design exercise. Improve message hierarchy, CTA clarity, proof placement, and scanability without inventing unsupported claims or requiring a net-new design system.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before recommending major redesigns, introducing new legal or billing language, or making public commitments that the product does not yet support.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when optimization depends on missing analytics wiring, unavailable product proof, or a broader brand redesign rather than page-level improvement.

roles.name: UI/UX Expert
roles.description: Owns product interface quality, interaction design, and design-system coherence so UI/UX work becomes clear, consistent, implementation-ready, and improved as a repeatable system instead of one-off screen polish.
roles.systemPrompt: Treat UI/UX as product-operating quality, not visual polish alone. Start from the user's goal, the current flow, and the friction or ambiguity in the interface. Improve structure, interaction patterns, state handling, and component consistency in ways that fit the current product and codebase. Favor clarity, predictable behavior, reuse, and implementation-ready guidance over novelty, and separate product UX decisions from marketing messaging or brand exploration.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before recommending a broad redesign, replacing a core navigation model, or introducing a net-new design system that would materially change multiple product surfaces.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when the real issue is messaging or brand positioning rather than product UX, when user goals or constraints are unclear, or when proposed improvements depend on research, instrumentation, or staffing that does not yet exist.

roles.name: UI/UX Implementation Specialist
roles.description: Implements approved UI/UX changes in product code so interaction intent, component behavior, state handling, and design-system patterns are translated into maintainable interface code instead of staying at the recommendation layer.
roles.systemPrompt: Treat this role as the code-facing execution partner for product UI/UX work. Implement approved or clearly implied UX intent in the codebase using existing patterns wherever possible, keep interaction behavior and state transitions explicit, and prefer reusable component and layout changes over one-off hacks. Do not broaden the task into product strategy or visual exploration unless the implementation is blocked by a missing decision. Preserve design-system coherence, watch for responsiveness and perceived performance regressions, and leave the result ready for review and iteration.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before replacing a major component pattern, introducing a new UI framework or design-system layer, or turning an implementation task into a broad product redesign.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when design intent is missing or contradictory, when implementation would require broader product UX decisions, when the safest fix depends on backend or data-model changes outside the UI surface, or when the requested change conflicts with established patterns that should be reviewed first.

roles.name: Role Advisor
roles.description: Advises on role selection and role-catalog gaps for tasks, goals, and workflows so work is routed to the best existing role when possible and new roles are proposed only when a real durable responsibility gap exists.
roles.systemPrompt: Treat role advice as a routing decision, not as catalog authoring or work coordination. Inspect the current role catalog, the work's intended outcome, and the nearest existing roles first. Prefer recommending an existing role with explicit reasoning, and only recommend creating a new role when the gap is durable, repeatable, and not better solved by a skill, provider, execution-surface, or task-instruction change. When a new role is warranted, define the missing responsibility boundary and nearest overlaps clearly enough for AMS Role Creator to implement without guessing.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before turning an advisory recommendation into a new role, recommending retirement of an existing role, or changing default routing expectations across tasks, goals, or workflows.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when the work to route is too vague to infer owned outcomes, when the current role catalog is incomplete or unavailable, when nearby roles overlap too heavily to distinguish cleanly, or when the real issue appears to be missing skills, providers, or execution-surface support rather than role selection.

roles.name: Codebase Maintenance Analyst
roles.description: Defines safe change boundaries, triages cleanup and refactor candidates, and reviews maintenance proof so codebase upkeep work stays evidence-backed, scoped, and low-risk.
roles.systemPrompt: Treat maintenance work as a safety and evidence problem, not as a generic refactor urge. Start by mapping the live product surface, runtime entry points, protected flows, and technical constraints that make removal or simplification risky. When reviewing detector output, separate provably safe cleanup from ambiguous candidates, scar tissue, and work that is messy but still live. Choose the smallest coherent batch that can be verified credibly, make risks and proof steps explicit, and after implementation inspect whether the claimed cleanup actually held without weakening protected behavior. Prefer preserving traceable safety over maximizing deletion volume.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before recommending removal or major simplification of protected routes, APIs, runtime entry points, auth/session behavior, persistence boundaries, or dynamically referenced surfaces.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when dynamic or framework-driven usage cannot be proven safe, when cleanup candidates cross auth, persistence, execution, or deployment boundaries, or when the real issue is broader redesign rather than maintenance-safe pruning.

roles.name: Tutorial Guide Author
roles.description: Owns learning-oriented documentation that leads a reader through one concrete working path so they gain confidence by completing the task themselves rather than by reading abstract explanation alone.
roles.systemPrompt: Teach by guiding the reader through one reliable happy path from setup to a visible success state. Optimize for learning by doing, safe defaults, explicit checkpoints, and momentum. Keep prerequisites minimal, make expected results visible after each major step, and avoid turning the tutorial into exhaustive reference material or a conceptual essay.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before replacing canonical onboarding flows, publishing tutorial steps that imply unsupported behavior, or recommending destructive actions as the default path.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when the product does not yet support one reliable happy path, when setup branches too widely for a single guided flow, or when missing examples make the tutorial speculative.

roles.name: How-To Guide Author
roles.description: Owns goal-oriented procedural guides for readers who already know what they want to accomplish and need the fastest reliable path, prerequisites, and recovery notes to get there.
roles.systemPrompt: Write for readers who already know the outcome they want and need the quickest trustworthy path to it. Start from the desired end state, make prerequisites and decision points explicit, keep explanations brief, and include only the variants or troubleshooting that materially affect completion. Do not broaden the guide into a tutorial, conceptual overview, or exhaustive reference page.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before changing canonical operating procedures, documenting risky admin actions as normal paths, or publishing unsupported workflows as recommended practice.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when the desired outcome is ambiguous, when environment-specific branching would make one guide misleading, or when the guide depends on undocumented or unstable behavior.

roles.name: Reference Documentation Author
roles.description: Owns lookup-first documentation for interfaces, commands, entities, fields, options, and policies so exact details stay structured, consistent, and trustworthy when readers need precision rather than narrative.
roles.systemPrompt: Produce lookup-first documentation where exactness matters more than persuasion. Optimize for consistency, completeness within scope, stable headings, and copy-paste-safe examples. Define terms precisely, distinguish required from optional values, and avoid tutorial pacing or conceptual digressions. If interface behavior is not verified, mark the uncertainty explicitly instead of filling gaps with plausible guesses.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before redefining canonical interfaces, deprecating documented commands or fields, or publishing reference claims that have not been verified against the actual system.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when interface behavior is ambiguous or changing, when the source of truth is split across code and docs, or when exact examples cannot be verified safely.

roles.name: Explanation Writer
roles.description: Owns conceptual documentation that explains why a system works the way it does, what tradeoffs matter, and how key ideas relate so readers can reason beyond a single procedure.
roles.systemPrompt: Explain why, not just what to click or type. Use mental models, contrasts, and cause-and-effect reasoning to clarify how the system works, what tradeoffs shape it, and where the boundaries are. Separate verified mechanics from interpretation, keep terminology consistent, and avoid collapsing explanation into tutorial steps, product marketing, or exhaustive reference tables.
roles.approvalPolicy: Require operator approval before publishing explanations that redefine architectural intent, policy rationale, or other decisions that should stay aligned with current owners.
roles.escalationPolicy: Escalate when the underlying rationale is undocumented or disputed, when multiple competing explanations exist, or when the requested explanation would overstate certainty about unsettled decisions.

workflows.name: App Review and Cleanup
workflows.summary: Reusable workflow template for reviewing an app codebase, separating definitely dead artifacts from risky or still-live complexity, removing one proven-safe cleanup batch at a time, and verifying the protected surface still works afterward.

workflows.name: Feature Development
workflows.summary: Reusable workflow template for turning a feature idea into a shipped product change with explicit planning, implementation, cleanup, documentation, and release steps.

workflows.name: UI/UX Review and Improvement
workflows.summary: Reusable workflow template for scoping a UI/UX problem, assessing the current experience, shaping the smallest useful improvement, implementing it, and validating whether the change actually improved the target flow.

workflows.name: Lead Discovery and Contact Sourcing
workflows.summary: Reusable workflow template for turning product truth into target segments, named organizations or people, and public contact paths with explicit fit reasoning and provenance.

workflowSteps.title: Protected Surface and Cleanup Scope
workflowSteps.summary: Define what must keep working, which routes and flows are actively relied on, which dynamic or framework-driven areas need extra caution, and what class of cleanup this pass is actually targeting before any removal starts.

workflowSteps.title: Objective Signal Sweep
workflowSteps.summary: Run the existing cleanup detectors and technical checks to produce an evidence-backed candidate list of unused files, exports, dependencies, suspicious assignments, stale feature remnants, and other review targets instead of guessing from the tree.

workflowSteps.title: Findings Classification and Batch Plan
workflowSteps.summary: Classify the findings into safe-to-remove, likely dead but needs manual verification, live but messy, or currently valuable scar tissue, then choose the smallest coherent cleanup batch with explicit risks and verification steps.

workflowSteps.title: Execute One Cleanup Batch
workflowSteps.summary: Remove or simplify only the selected batch, update the affected imports, exports, or configuration, and keep the change narrow enough that any breakage can be attributed and reversed quickly.

workflowSteps.title: Review Proof and Capture Follow-Up
workflowSteps.summary: Check that the chosen verification signals passed, confirm the protected surface still holds, record any false positives or dynamic-usage caveats, and call out the next cleanup candidate instead of declaring the whole app cleaned.

workflowSteps.title: Requirements Gathering
workflowSteps.summary: Clarify the operator need, scope boundaries, constraints, and success signal for the feature before design or implementation starts.

workflowSteps.title: Feature Draft and Design
workflowSteps.summary: Turn the validated requirement into a concrete MVP plan, interface direction, and implementation-ready shape.

workflowSteps.title: Technical Implementation
workflowSteps.summary: Implement the feature in the codebase and get it into a working state that satisfies the approved design and scope.

workflowSteps.title: UI/UX Improvements and Cleanup
workflowSteps.summary: Refine rough edges, tighten the interaction flow, and clean up interface or usability issues after the main implementation exists.

workflowSteps.title: Documentation Writing and Update
workflowSteps.summary: Update the relevant product, operator, or developer documentation so the new feature is understandable and maintainable.

workflowSteps.title: Release
workflowSteps.summary: Validate release readiness, confirm documentation and cleanup are complete, and ship the change with appropriate coordination.

workflowSteps.title: Consequence Framing
workflowSteps.summary: Turn the current product truth into a short consequence map that makes explicit which real-world problems the product can credibly reduce, who experiences those problems, and which claims are still assumptions.

workflowSteps.title: Pain-Bearing Segment Ranking
workflowSteps.summary: Identify which industries, organization types, teams, or roles are most likely to feel those consequences sharply enough to act, and rank them with explicit reasoning instead of broad market guesses.

workflowSteps.title: Named Lead and Account Sourcing
workflowSteps.summary: Translate the ranked segments into named organizations, potential users, buyers, or stakeholder roles, and record why each candidate appears to fit instead of collecting a generic list.

workflowSteps.title: Public Contact Path Mapping
workflowSteps.summary: Capture where each lead or segment can actually be reached, including public contact routes, directories, communities, or platforms, and separate direct contacts from broader search venues for follow-up sourcing.

workflowSteps.title: Review Target Framing
workflowSteps.summary: Clarify the product surface, target user or job, current friction, constraints, and what a better outcome should mean before reviewing screens or proposing fixes.

workflowSteps.title: Current-State UI/UX Assessment
workflowSteps.summary: Inspect the current flow, capture concrete usability and presentation problems, and where useful compare a small set of adjacent patterns so confirmed issues are separated from taste or guesswork.

workflowSteps.title: Improvement Slice Design
workflowSteps.summary: Turn the findings into the smallest useful improvement plan with interaction-model decisions, visual structure, non-goals, and implementation boundaries that can actually be built next.

workflowSteps.title: Implement Selected Changes
workflowSteps.summary: Apply the approved interface and behavior changes in the codebase while keeping scope tied to the selected improvement slice instead of letting the review expand into a redesign.

workflowSteps.title: Validate Result and Decide Follow-Up
workflowSteps.summary: Re-run the target flow after implementation, confirm whether the original problems were actually reduced, and call out residual issues, regressions, or next-step candidates before closing the cycle.

taskTemplates.name: Report Draft
taskTemplates.summary: Reusable intake template for drafting structured reports, summaries, and operator-facing writeups.
taskTemplates.taskSummary: Draft a structured report that summarizes the requested material clearly, highlights the most important findings or decisions, and is ready for review and reuse.
taskTemplates.successCriteria: The report is clear, well-structured, complete enough for its audience, and does not require major rewriting before review.
taskTemplates.readyCondition: The source material, intended audience, and purpose of the report are all clear enough to draft against.
taskTemplates.expectedOutcome: A review-ready report draft with a usable structure, clear conclusions, and any needed supporting context.

taskTemplates.name: Role Creation
taskTemplates.summary: Reusable intake template for defining a new role with responsibilities, capabilities, tools, and prompt guidance.
taskTemplates.taskSummary: Define the new role clearly enough that it can be added to the system with a useful description, scope, capabilities, tools, quality checklist, and any role-specific prompt guidance.
taskTemplates.successCriteria: The role is clearly scoped, differentiated from existing roles, and ready to be implemented without ambiguity.
taskTemplates.readyCondition: The need for the role is understood, including the types of tasks it should own and how it differs from existing roles.
taskTemplates.expectedOutcome: A complete role definition ready to be created in the system and used in task routing.

taskTemplates.name: Research Brief
taskTemplates.summary: Reusable intake template for repeatable research requests in the AMS prototype project.
taskTemplates.taskSummary: Investigate the target topic, gather credible sources, synthesize the strongest findings, and produce a concise report that can be reused for planning or implementation decisions.
taskTemplates.successCriteria: The output identifies the most relevant findings, cites the strongest available sources, and makes the implications for the project clear.
taskTemplates.readyCondition: The research question, intended audience, and decision this work should support are all clear enough to investigate.
taskTemplates.expectedOutcome: A usable research memo or report with credible sources, synthesized findings, and practical implications.

tasks.title: Feature Task: In-App Assistant for Agent Management System
tasks.summary:
Feature Name

In-App Context-Aware Assistant for Structured System Actions

Summary

Implement an always-available assistant inside the agent management system that allows the user to interact with the system using natural language and, eventually, voice input. The assistant should understand requests like creating goals, tasks, roles, agents, workflows, or other records, ask for clarification when needed, and convert user intent into structured actions within the system.

The assistant should also be context-aware, meaning it should have access to relevant information about what the user is currently viewing or interacting with in the application. This should help it interpret instructions more accurately, infer relationships more intelligently, reduce unnecessary clarification questions, and perform more relevant actions.

The purpose of this feature is to reduce friction when using the agent management system by allowing the user to simply tell the system what they want created or changed instead of manually navigating forms and screens for every action.

⸻

Problem

The current system likely requires too much manual interaction to create or manage structured records such as tasks, goals, roles, and instructions. This adds friction, especially when the user already knows what they want and could describe it much faster through speech or text.

A generic assistant without awareness of the current application state would still force the user to over-explain things that the system should already know. If the user is on a specific project page, workflow page, agent detail page, role detail page, or task list, the assistant should use that context to better understand what “this,” “here,” “under this,” “related to this,” or “add one for this” refers to.

The system therefore needs a faster, more natural interface layer that sits on top of the structured data model and current UI context and can interpret user intent accordingly.

Examples of friction this should solve:

* User has to manually open forms and fill fields one by one
* User has to remember where in the system a record type lives
* User has to translate thoughts into the system’s structure manually
* User has to repeat context the app already knows
* Voice-based idea capture and action entry are not smoothly supported
* The assistant may misinterpret intent without awareness of the current page or selected object

⸻

Desired Outcome

The user should be able to open an assistant panel and say or type things like:

* “Create a goal for launching the onboarding prototype.”
* “Make a task called ‘Create web developer role.’”
* “Create a role called Web Developer with instructions saying it is an expert in web development, frontend architecture, responsive UI, and debugging.”
* “Add a task under this project for redesigning the workflow page.”
* “Create an agent based on this role.”
* “Make this into a feature request.”
* “Add a child task for this.”
* “What related tasks already exist here?”

The assistant should:

1. Understand the request
2. Use current UI/page context to interpret references and intent
3. Infer the intended object type when possible
4. Extract structured fields
5. Ask follow-up questions only when required
6. Show the user a preview of what it plans to create or do
7. Let the user confirm, edit, or cancel
8. Create the record(s) in the system

⸻

Primary Goal

Create a usable, context-aware assistant interface that can translate conversational input into structured system actions for the agent management system.

⸻

Scope for V1

V1 should focus on a narrow but useful version of the feature.

V1 should support:

* Text input to the assistant
* Optional paste-in transcript input
* Interpreting requests to create:
    * Tasks
    * Goals
    * Roles
    * Agents
* Structured field extraction from natural language
* Awareness of current page, selected object, and relevant local context
* Preview before save
* Confirmation before record creation
* Basic clarification flow when required
* Error handling when intent is unclear
* Logging of assistant actions, context used, and created records

V1 should not try to fully solve:

* Complex multi-step workflow orchestration
* Fully autonomous execution of broad system changes
* Advanced voice/audio recording pipeline if that adds too much complexity
* Deep cross-object reasoning beyond straightforward create/update flows
* Open-ended destructive actions without confirmation

⸻

Context Awareness Requirement

Definition

The assistant should be aware of relevant application context at the time the user submits a request. This means it should receive structured information about what the user is currently viewing or interacting with so that it can interpret the request more accurately.

This context may include:

* current page or route
* current object type
* current object ID
* currently viewed record details
* selected item(s)
* parent object or breadcrumb context
* surrounding list/filter/search state if relevant
* current project/workflow/role/agent/task the user is inside
* available actions valid for the current context

Examples

If the user is on:

* a project page, “create a task for this” should default to creating a task linked to that project
* a role detail page, “create an agent for this” should infer that “this” refers to the current role
* a workflow page, “add a step about validation” should infer the workflow as parent context
* a task detail page, “make this blocked” or “add a child task” should use that task as the target
* an agent list page, “create one for documentation” should infer that the user likely means an agent, not some unrelated object type

The assistant should reduce ambiguity by using available context, but still show a preview before committing actions.

⸻

Functional Requirements

1. Assistant Entry Point

Implement an assistant that is easy to access from anywhere in the agent management system.

Possible UX patterns:

* Persistent floating assistant button
* Slide-over panel
* Global command bar with assistant mode
* Dedicated assistant page with chat history

Minimum requirement:

* Assistant can be opened from the main application UI without deep navigation

⸻

2. Input Modes

V1

* Text input
* Support for pasted voice transcripts

V2+

* Direct voice recording
* Speech-to-text transcription
* Optional playback and transcript editing before submit

⸻

3. Context Injection

When the assistant is opened or when the user submits a request, the system should provide the assistant with structured context about the current UI state.

This context should be passed in a controlled, structured form rather than forcing the assistant to infer everything from raw rendered text.

Example context payload:

{
  "route": "/projects/123/tasks",
  "pageType": "project_detail",
  "currentObject": {
    "type": "project",
    "id": "123",
    "name": "Onboarding Prototype"
  },
  "selectedObjects": [],
  "breadcrumbs": [
    { "type": "workspace", "id": "w1", "name": "AdventureWare" },
    { "type": "project", "id": "123", "name": "Onboarding Prototype" }
  ],
  "visibleCapabilities": [
    "create_task",
    "create_goal",
    "view_project",
    "link_existing_task"
  ]
}

The assistant should use this context to improve intent recognition and payload generation.

⸻

4. Intent Recognition

The assistant should classify what the user is trying to do.

Initial supported intents:

* Create task
* Create goal
* Create role
* Create agent
* Ask system question
* Request clarification/help

Intent recognition should consider:

* the user’s raw input
* the current page context
* the currently viewed object
* the kinds of actions that are valid in the current location

The system should infer likely intent from phrasing and context, but not rely on exact keywords.

⸻

5. Structured Field Extraction

The assistant should extract relevant fields from the user’s natural-language input.

Example mappings

Task

* name/title
* description/instructions
* parent object/project if mentioned or inferred from context
* priority if mentioned
* tags if useful
* status default

Goal

* name/title
* description
* timeframe if mentioned
* parent area/project if mentioned or inferred from context

Role

* name/title
* description
* instructions
* specialty/focus areas

Agent

* name/title
* linked role if mentioned or inferred from context
* instructions
* capabilities or tools if supported
* purpose

The assistant should populate as much as possible automatically, including context-derived defaults, then present a draft.

⸻

6. Clarification Flow

If the user gives incomplete or ambiguous instructions, the assistant should ask focused follow-up questions.

Examples:

* “Do you want this created as a task or a goal?”
* “Should this role also include backend development, or just frontend/web?”
* “Should I attach this to the current project?”
* “Do you want me to create the role only, or also create an agent that uses it?”

Clarification should be minimal and only used when required after considering available context.

⸻

7. Preview Before Commit

Before saving, the assistant should show a structured preview of what it plans to create.

Example:

Create Role

* Name: Web Developer
* Description: Expert in web development
* Instructions: Specializes in frontend architecture, responsive UI, debugging, and web app implementation
* Context Used: Current page = Roles, Parent workspace = AdventureWare

Actions:

* Confirm
* Edit
* Cancel

This is important because the assistant may interpret things imperfectly and should not silently create bad records.

⸻

8. Record Creation

After confirmation, the assistant should create the record in the system using the same underlying creation logic used by standard forms or APIs.

Important:

* Assistant-created records should use the same validation rules as manually created records
* No special inconsistent backend path
* Reuse domain services where possible
* Context-derived relationships should still be validated before commit

⸻

9. Action Log / Traceability

Every assistant action should be logged.

Track:

* raw user input
* interpreted intent
* extracted fields
* context snapshot used for interpretation
* follow-up questions asked
* final confirmed payload
* result of creation
* errors if any

This will help debugging, trust, refinement, and prompt iteration.

⸻

10. Safety / Guardrails

The assistant should not perform destructive or high-risk actions without clear confirmation.

For V1:

* Focus primarily on create flows
* If update/delete is added later, always require explicit confirmation
* If the assistant is uncertain, it should say so instead of guessing aggressively
* Context should improve confidence, not justify unsafe silent actions

⸻

UX Requirements

Core UX Principles

* Fast
* Low friction
* Clear
* Reversible
* Trustworthy
* Structured without feeling rigid
* Context-aware without feeling opaque

Desired Feel

The assistant should feel like a practical operator layer for the system, not like a generic chatbot. It should be specifically good at understanding the objects, workflows, and current application context of the agent management system.

UX Flow Example

1. User opens assistant
2. User types or pastes transcript
3. Assistant receives current page/context metadata
4. Assistant interprets request using both user input and app context
5. Assistant asks follow-up only if necessary
6. Assistant shows a structured draft
7. User confirms
8. Assistant creates record
9. Assistant returns success message and link to the new record

⸻

Example User Stories

Story 1

As a user, I want to tell the assistant to create a task in plain English so I do not have to manually fill out a task form.

Story 2

As a user, I want the assistant to understand what page or record I am currently viewing so I do not have to restate obvious context.

Story 3

As a user, I want to dictate a role description by voice transcript so I can capture ideas quickly when thinking out loud.

Story 4

As a user, I want the assistant to convert my messy phrasing into structured fields so the system remains organized without slowing me down.

Story 5

As a user, I want to review and confirm what the assistant plans to create before it saves anything.

Story 6

As a user, I want the assistant to understand the system’s objects and my current location well enough that it feels like a specialized operator, not a generic chat box.

⸻

Example Inputs and Expected Behavior

Example 1

Context: User is on project “Onboarding Prototype”
Input:
“Create a task called Create Web Developer Role. Instructions are to create a web developer role that is an expert at web development.”

Expected behavior:
Assistant identifies intent = create task
Extracts:

* name = Create Web Developer Role
* instructions = Create a web developer role that is an expert at web development
* parent project = Onboarding Prototype (from context)

Shows preview, then creates task on confirmation.

⸻

Example 2

Context: User is on role detail page for “Frontend Engineer”
Input:
“Create an agent for this.”

Expected behavior:
Assistant identifies intent = create agent
Infers linked role = Frontend Engineer from context
Shows preview
Creates agent on confirmation

⸻

Example 3

Context: User is on workflow page
Input:
“I need something to track improving this page.”

Expected behavior:
Assistant uses current page context to infer likely relation to workflow or feature work
Still detects ambiguity between task/goal/feature request
Asks:
“Do you want this created as a task, a goal, or a feature request?”

⸻

Technical Implementation Requirements

Architecture Guidance

Build this as a thin assistant layer on top of the existing domain model and create/update services.

Do not hardcode brittle UI-only logic if the same operations already exist in backend/domain services.

Recommended architecture:

1. Assistant UI
2. Context provider / context serializer
3. Assistant orchestration layer
4. Intent parsing / field extraction layer
5. Domain action layer
6. Logging/audit layer

⸻

Suggested System Components

1. Assistant UI Component

Responsible for:

* rendering conversation/messages
* input box
* submission
* preview cards
* clarification prompts
* confirm/edit/cancel actions

2. Context Provider

Responsible for:

* reading current route and UI state
* identifying current object and parent object
* gathering selected/active entities
* exposing context in a clean structured format
* limiting context to useful, relevant information

3. Intent + Extraction Layer

Responsible for:

* interpreting raw natural language
* classifying object type and action type
* using context to improve interpretation
* extracting structured fields
* returning confidence + missing fields

This can initially be rule-assisted and/or LLM-assisted depending on the prototype approach.

4. Action Planner

Responsible for converting interpreted intent plus context into a system action plan like:

{
  "action": "create_task",
  "payload": {
    "name": "Create Web Developer Role",
    "instructions": "Create a web developer role that is an expert at web development.",
    "projectId": "123"
  },
  "contextUsed": {
    "pageType": "project_detail",
    "currentObject": {
      "type": "project",
      "id": "123",
      "name": "Onboarding Prototype"
    }
  },
  "missingFields": [],
  "needsConfirmation": true
}

5. Domain Executor

Responsible for:

* validating payload
* calling actual create services
* returning created object metadata

6. Audit Log

Responsible for:

* storing transcript/input
* context snapshot
* interpreted action
* payload
* outcome

⸻

Recommended V1 Design Strategy

Keep V1 narrow and reliable

Do not try to make it an all-knowing general assistant yet.

Instead, make it very good at:

* understanding a handful of object types
* creating records reliably
* using current app context intelligently
* showing previews
* asking minimal clarifying questions

Prefer a structured intermediate representation

Do not let the assistant directly mutate the database from freeform chat output.

Always convert assistant output into a structured action plan first.

Reuse existing create logic

If tasks, goals, roles, and agents already have forms/services, the assistant should call into those instead of duplicating business logic.

Make context explicit

Do not rely on vague hidden magic. Keep context gathering explicit, inspectable, and debuggable.

Make it debug-friendly

You will want to inspect:

* what the user said
* what context the assistant received
* what the assistant thought they meant
* why it asked a question
* what payload it produced

⸻

Acceptance Criteria

Minimum acceptance criteria for V1

* User can open assistant from the app UI
* User can enter natural language text
* Assistant receives current page/context metadata
* Assistant can interpret requests to create tasks, goals, roles, or agents
* Assistant can use current context to improve interpretation and linking
* Assistant can extract key fields from input
* Assistant asks for clarification when intent is insufficiently clear
* Assistant shows a preview before creation
* User can confirm or cancel
* Record is created successfully in the system after confirmation
* Assistant-generated actions are logged, including context used
* Error states are handled cleanly

⸻

Non-Functional Requirements

Performance

* Assistant interaction should feel responsive
* Preview generation should happen quickly enough to feel conversational

Reliability

* Assistant should fail safely
* Ambiguous cases should not silently create incorrect records

Maintainability

* Assistant action types should be modular and extensible
* New object types and new context providers should be easy to add later

Usability

* The feature should reduce friction, not create more
* The assistant must be simpler than manual form entry for common cases

⸻

Nice-to-Have Features After V1

* Voice recording button with transcription
* Context-aware suggestions based on current page
* “Create from this page”
* “Turn this into a task”
* Edit assistant-created payload before save
* Suggested field autofill
* Multi-record creation from one input
* Search existing records before creating duplicates
* Links to created objects
* In-session contextual memory
* Deeper page-aware assistant actions beyond create flows

⸻

Risks / Challenges

* Overly broad assistant scope could make implementation messy
* Misclassification of user intent could create bad data
* Poor context modeling could cause confusing behavior
* Trying to support too many object types too early could slow progress
* Voice support can become a distraction if included too early
* Trust will drop quickly if the assistant feels generic or inaccurate

⸻

Recommended Phased Implementation

Phase 1

Implement text-only assistant for create flows:

* create task
* create goal
* create role
* create agent

Include:

* current page awareness
* current object awareness
* structured preview and confirmation

Phase 2

Improve disambiguation and context awareness:

* infer parent project/context more reliably
* suggest links to related records
* reduce follow-up questions
* support selected object context

Phase 3

Add voice input:

* record audio
* transcribe
* edit transcript before submit

Phase 4

Support update/search/query flows:

* “show me tasks related to this”
* “update this role”
* “mark this task as blocked”

⸻

Deliverables

The implementation should result in:

1. An assistant UI in the agent management system
2. Natural-language-to-structured-action parsing for V1 object types
3. Context injection from current UI/page state
4. Preview and confirmation flow
5. Record creation integration
6. Action logging
7. Basic documentation for how the assistant works and how to extend it

⸻

Updated Instruction Block for Codex

Implement a V1 in-app context-aware assistant for the agent management system.
Goal:
Create an always-available assistant UI that allows the user to type natural-language instructions and have the system interpret them into structured actions for creating records such as tasks, goals, roles, and agents. The assistant must also use current application context so it can better understand what the user is looking at and interpret instructions more accurately.
Requirements:
- Add an assistant entry point accessible from the main app UI
- Support text input in V1
- Allow users to enter instructions like:
  - “Create a task called Create Web Developer Role”
  - “Create a role named Web Developer that is an expert in frontend development”
  - “Create a goal for improving onboarding”
  - “Create an agent for this”
  - “Add a task under this project”
- Build a context provider that passes structured current-page/app-state context into the assistant, including where possible:
  - current route
  - page type
  - current object type/id/name
  - selected objects
  - parent/breadcrumb context
  - valid actions for the current screen
- Build an interpretation layer that:
  - identifies user intent
  - determines the target object type
  - uses current context to improve interpretation
  - extracts structured fields from freeform input
  - identifies missing required fields
- Before creating anything, show a preview of the interpreted result and require user confirmation
- If intent or required fields are ambiguous, ask a focused clarification question
- After confirmation, create the record using the system’s normal domain/service layer rather than duplicating creation logic
- Log the raw input, context snapshot, interpreted action, extracted payload, and final result for debugging/audit purposes
V1 scope:
- Create flows only
- Supported object types: task, goal, role, agent
- No destructive actions
- No full autonomous multi-step workflows yet
- Voice can be deferred, but structure the code so voice transcript input can be added later
Implementation guidance:
- Keep the assistant architecture modular
- Add a dedicated context provider / serializer layer
- Use a structured intermediate action plan before executing any operation
- Reuse existing validation and creation services
- Optimize for reliability and clarity over cleverness
- Make context usage explicit and debuggable
- Make it easy to extend with more action types later
Deliverables:
- Assistant UI
- Context provider
- Intent/extraction layer
- Preview and confirmation flow
- Record creation integration
- Logging/audit trail
- Clear code organization and brief implementation notes

⸻

Shorter Updated Task Version

Title:
Implement context-aware in-app assistant for agent management system

Description:
Add an always-available assistant to the agent management system that lets the user create structured records such as tasks, goals, roles, and agents using natural language. The assistant should use both the user’s input and the current application context, such as the page being viewed and the current object, to interpret requests more accurately. It should extract relevant fields, ask clarification questions when needed, show a preview before saving, and create records through the normal system logic. Design V1 around text input first, but keep the architecture ready for future voice/transcript input.

Acceptance Criteria:

* Assistant accessible from main UI
* Supports text-based natural language input
* Receives current page/object context
* Can create tasks, goals, roles, and agents
* Uses context to improve interpretation and linking
* Extracts fields from freeform input
* Asks clarifying questions when needed
* Shows preview before create
* Requires confirmation
* Uses existing creation logic
* Logs assistant actions, including context used
