tasks.title: Improve thread reuse for Improve UI responsiveness and mobile appearance
tasks.summary:
Review why the system identified a better reusable thread for this task. Suggested thread: Yeah — what you’re describing is not just “a place to launch agents.” It’s really an **operations system for converting goals into continuously executed work through humans + AI**.

The key thing is that you’re already circling the real answer: the purpose of an agent management system is not “manage agents” as an end in itself. The agents are not the product. They’re labor capacity. The real purpose is to **direct, monitor, and compound useful work**.

So if I were to distill your thinking into a cleaner core:

**An agent management system exists to keep available cognitive labor pointed at the highest-value work with the least friction, the least downtime, and the right amount of human oversight.**

That is the point.

What you are building is basically a hybrid of:

* agent orchestration
* work management
* oversight / approvals
* execution routing
* capacity management
* operational visibility

In other words, it is closer to a **mission control system for AI work** than just a “chat with agents” app.

## The real goal

Your goal is probably something like this:

**Create a system where useful work keeps moving forward, even when you are not sitting at your laptop, by giving AI agents structured work, monitoring their progress, intervening only when needed, and ensuring their effort stays aligned with real goals.**

That covers the main things you kept hitting:

* reduce downtime
* keep agents busy on meaningful tasks
* make work continue when you’re away
* maintain visibility and control
* avoid random disconnected output
* connect execution to goals and priorities
* route labor where it is most useful

That is a much stronger framing than “I want to run Codex from my phone.”

Running Codex remotely is a feature.
Keeping a work system alive beyond your physical presence is the deeper objective.

## The deeper problem it solves

Without a system like this, AI usage is usually very fragmented:

You think of a task.
You manually prompt an agent.
You wait.
You check back later.
You copy results somewhere.
You decide what’s next.
You lose continuity.
The agent sits idle when you’re gone.
Work stalls anytime you’re unavailable.

So the system is trying to solve a real operational problem:

**AI labor is cheap and flexible, but it is underutilized because task selection, coordination, visibility, approvals, and continuity are still mostly manual.**

Your system is meant to close that gap.

## What it is not

It helps to define what this thing is *not*, because otherwise it can bloat fast.

It is not primarily:

* a generic chatbot wrapper
* a novelty multi-agent simulator
* a dashboard full of fake intelligence theater
* just another task manager
* just a remote terminal for Codex
* an autonomous system with no human governance

Those may overlap with it, but the center of gravity should stay on this:

**turning available AI capacity into reliable forward progress on meaningful work**

## The core functions

If you strip it down, the system probably needs to do five things really well.

### 1. Represent goals and priorities

Agents should not just consume prompts. They should consume work that exists in context.

That means the system needs some structure for:

* goals
* projects
* tasks
* priorities
* dependencies
* status
* risk / confidence
* expected outcome

Because otherwise the agents are just being fed one-off instructions with no larger operational logic.

This is why your instinct that it needs work management built in is correct. Not because you want to recreate Jira for fun, but because agents need a **work substrate**.

Without that substrate, there is no real autonomy. There is only manual prompting.

### 2. Convert work into executable units

A human goal is usually too vague for an agent.

So the system needs to help bridge:

* mission -> project
* project -> objective
* objective -> task
* task -> executable agent assignment

That translation layer is crucial.

For example:

* Goal: Improve product quality
* Project: Review navigation flows
* Task: Audit mobile add-item flow for state loss, friction, and inconsistency
* Agent instruction: inspect routes/components/state handling, produce findings, propose fixes, estimate risk

That conversion is a huge part of the value.

### 3. Dispatch and coordinate agent labor

This is the obvious part, but it matters.

The system needs to be able to:

* start agent sessions
* assign work
* track current activity
* capture outputs
* know when an agent is blocked
* know when it’s done
* know when it needs approval
* potentially hand work off to another agent or queue the next task

This is the shop floor layer you were describing.

Not “AI magic,” but operational routing:
who is working on what, with what context, at what cost, under what constraints.

### 4. Keep humans in the loop only where needed

This is probably one of the most important design questions.

You do **not** want maximum autonomy in all cases. You want the right autonomy for the task.

So the system should eventually be able to distinguish between:

* safe to auto-run
* safe to auto-continue
* needs human review before execution
* needs approval for destructive or costly action
* blocked and awaiting clarification
* escalated because confidence is low

That is the real governance model.

A good agent system is not one that removes humans completely. It is one that **uses human attention sparingly and precisely**.

Your attention is a scarce resource. The system should protect it.

### 5. Preserve continuity and operational awareness

This is the “I want to know what’s happening while I’m away” layer.

The system should let you quickly answer:

* What is currently running?
* What finished?
* What is blocked?
* What needs my approval?
* What capacity is idle?
* What is highest priority right now?
* What tasks can be safely picked up next?
* Which agent sessions are producing value versus thrashing?

This matters a lot because otherwise AI work becomes opaque and annoying.

If you have to dig through raw transcripts every time, the system is failing.

## A good one-sentence definition

You could define it like this:

**An agent management system is a goal- and task-driven operations layer that coordinates AI and human labor to keep meaningful work moving with visibility, control, and minimal downtime.**

Or even more bluntly:

**It is a work orchestration system for AI agents.**

## The essential mental model

I think the best mental model is:

### AI agents are workers

### tasks are units of work

### goals determine what matters

### the system is operations management

### the human is director / approver / strategist

That framing avoids a lot of fluff.

It also makes design choices clearer. For example:

If an agent is like labor capacity, then you care about:

* utilization
* quality
* throughput
* queue health
* failure handling
* escalation rules
* idle time
* routing efficiency

Those are operations questions, not “AI vibes” questions.

## What success looks like

A successful system probably behaves something like this:

You define or import goals and priorities.
Those get broken into tasks and a backlog.
Tasks are labeled by type, difficulty, risk, required tools, and suitability for AI vs human.
Available agents pull or are assigned eligible tasks.
They execute with the necessary context.
They produce outputs, artifacts, summaries, and status changes.
If blocked, they request clarification in a structured way.
If high-risk, they pause for approval.
If complete, they either hand off to the next step or return for review.
You can monitor all of this from anywhere.
Your own work and the agents’ work live in the same operational picture.

That is a coherent product.

## What the MVP should probably optimize for

Since you said prototype, I would not try to build “full autonomous multi-agent civilization” right now.

The MVP should probably answer a smaller question:

**Can I create a system where a small number of agent sessions reliably pull from a prioritized work queue, do useful work, and notify me only when needed?**

That is enough.

If that works, you already have something real.

So the MVP likely needs:

### A work model

At minimum:

* goals
* projects
* tasks
* priority
* status
* assignee
* AI-suitable or not
* risk level
* required approval or not

### An agent/session model

At minimum:

* agent identity or type
* current task
* current status
* session history
* last heartbeat/activity
* provider/model/runtime info
* cost/token usage if available

### A queue + dispatch model

At minimum:

* tasks ready for work
* tasks blocked
* tasks awaiting review
* tasks completed
* rules for what agents can pick up

### A monitoring model

At minimum:

* active sessions
* idle capacity
* blocked items
* pending approvals
* recent outputs

### A review/approval model

At minimum:

* show me what needs a decision
* let me approve/reject from mobile
* preserve context so I do not need to reread everything

That alone is already a strong prototype.

## The most important design choice

The biggest architectural question is probably this:

**Is the center of the system the agent, or the work?**

I think it should be the **work**.

That means:

* agents are replaceable
* models are replaceable
* providers are replaceable
* sessions are temporary
* work continuity survives tool changes

If you center everything on Codex sessions, you’ll end up with a Codex cockpit.

If you center everything on goals/tasks/outputs/status, you’ll end up with a real operations layer that can use Codex today and something else tomorrow.

That is a much better foundation.

## A useful hierarchy

You may want a hierarchy something like this:

### Mission / Goals

Why this matters at all.

### Projects / Initiatives

Meaningful chunks of work in service of goals.

### Tasks

Discrete units of work.

### Runs / Assignments

A specific attempt by a specific agent or human to execute a task.

### Outputs / Artifacts

What got produced.

### Decisions / Approvals

Human interventions and governance checkpoints.

### Metrics / Telemetry

Utilization, time, cost, success rate, blockage rate, etc.

That hierarchy keeps things sane.

## Good principles for the system

A few principles seem important based on what you said.

### 1. Work should not depend on your immediate presence

If you step away, the system should still move.

### 2. Human attention should be used only where it adds real value

Do not make yourself the bottleneck for trivial things.

### 3. Agent effort must stay tied to actual goals

No endless busywork or detached outputs.

### 4. Visibility should be cheap

You should be able to glance and understand the state fast.

### 5. Execution should be resumable

If a session dies or a provider changes, work should survive.

### 6. The system should degrade gracefully

If autonomy fails, it should fall back to queues, status, and approvals rather than chaos.

### 7. Keep the model/provider layer abstracted

Do not weld the whole thing to one tool too early.

## A danger to watch for

The biggest trap is building an elaborate system that mostly manages AI activity rather than producing useful outcomes.

That usually happens when the product becomes obsessed with:

* agent personalities
* agent swarms
* complicated routing theatrics
* excessive autonomy logic
* dashboards that look impressive but don’t increase throughput or quality

You want to stay grounded in:
**Did meaningful work get done?**
That is the north star.

## A sharper product framing

If you wanted to articulate it in product language, maybe:

**This system turns AI agents from ad hoc prompt tools into an operational workforce that can be assigned, monitored, and kept aligned with real priorities.**

Or:

**It is the layer between strategic goals and available AI capacity.**

That second one is especially strong.

Because that really is what you’re building:
the interface between intention and execution.

## Practical requirement categories

If you want a cleaner requirements lens, I’d break them into these buckets:

### Strategic layer

* goals
* priorities
* planning
* backlog generation
* dependency awareness

### Execution layer

* task assignment
* agent session launch
* context injection
* runtime selection
* retries / handoffs

### Oversight layer

* status tracking
* approval queues
* alerts
* summaries
* logs / audit trail

### Capacity layer

* available agents
* model/provider constraints
* local vs cloud execution
* cost limits
* usage quotas
* scheduling

### Continuity layer

* persistent task state
* resumable runs
* artifact storage
* cross-device access
* mobile monitoring and intervention

That structure is probably closer to the real requirements than just “needs agent setup and tasks.”

## The ultimate purpose

At the highest level, I think your purpose is this:

**Build a system that lets intelligence — human and artificial — be organized as a coherent, continuously operating force toward meaningful goals.**

That sounds lofty, but that’s basically what you’re describing.

More concretely:

You want less stop-start friction.
Less idle capacity.
Less dependence on being at your laptop.
Less chaos and forgotten context.
More steady progress.
More leverage from the tools you already have.
More reliable translation from goals into completed work.

That is a real and worthwhile thing to build.

## My blunt recommendation

As you continue building, keep asking this question:

**Does this feature increase the system’s ability to turn available AI capacity into useful, goal-aligned output with low oversight cost?**

If yes, it probably belongs.

If not, it may be fluff, premature abstraction, or a side quest.

And for your prototype, I would make the target very simple:

**One backlog, a few agents, clear status states, approval checkpoints, mobile visibility, and basic auto-pickup of low-risk tasks.**

That alone would already demonstrate the essence of the system.

If you want, I can turn everything you just said into a cleaner product definition plus a structured set of MVP requirements and design principles you can build against.. Refine the thread-assignment rules so similar tasks inherit stronger context automatically.

tasks.title: Migrate App to Skeleton 4, and centralize design b/t app and website
tasks.summary:
I need Codex to review and help address inconsistencies between the Kwipoo app and the Kwipoo website, specifically around the design system and theming. Currently, the website is built on SvelteKit using Skeleton v4, along with its design system and Tailwind. The app, however, is still partially on Skeleton v3, with at least one or two components still coming from Skeleton v2 due to earlier issues during migration. One of the goals is to complete the migration of the app to Skeleton v4 so both the app and website are aligned on the same version and system. Beyond version alignment, the larger issue is inconsistency in the design system and brand theming across the app and website. Right now, colors, tokens, and styling are not well standardized, making it difficult to maintain consistency and update things efficiently. The goal is to centralize and standardize the design system so that theme values (colors, tokens, etc.) are consistent, reusable, and easy to manage across both the app and the website. I added a `Design References` folder in the project that includes a palette reference image (turquoise and gold). I want Codex to review this and use it as a basis for defining a more intentional and cohesive color theme for Kwipoo. This should align with the existing direction but be more deliberate and grounded in the reference instead of loosely or arbitrarily chosen values. Overall goals: * Complete migration of the app to Skeleton v4 * Eliminate legacy components from older Skeleton versions, don't do "shims", actually replace the components as necessary * Unify the design system between app and website * Define a consistent, centralized theming approach (colors, tokens, etc.) * Use the provided palette reference to refine and standardize brand colors * Make the system easier to maintain and update going forward Codex should review the current implementation in both the app and website, identify inconsistencies and technical debt related to styling/theming, and propose a clear, incremental plan to unify and standardize everything. Here's relevant instructions from Skeleton for reference, if needed: https://www.skeleton.dev/docs/svelte/get-started/migrate-from-v3.md # Migrate from v3 Learn how to migrate from Skeleton v3 to the latest release. Skeleton v4 introduces a top-to-bottom overhaul of the component APIs. The goal has been to stabilized the internal and external APIs of our component system and ensure we can continue to introduce new components and new component frameworks over time. It also aims to make Skeleton's components as simple and intuitive to use as possible for new users. ## Prerequisites We recommend you handle all migration changes on a dedicated feature branch. This ensures you can easily drop and revert if anything goes wrong. ```bash git checkout -b migration ``` Make sure you've accounted for the following: * Ensure both Skeleton packages (core and framework) are updated to the latest v3.x release. * Update all critical dependencies in your app to their latest version (optional but recommended) * Make sure your app has been tested and is in a functional state. *** ## Automated Migration To begin, we will run a quick automated migration. ```bash npx skeleton@latest migrate skeleton-4 ``` This will handle the package and stylesheet updates in the next couple steps. If you opt for the CLI, feel free to skip down to the [Manual Migration](/docs/\[framework]/get-started/migrate-from-v3#manual-migration) steps below. Note the manual steps are required for all migrations. ### Update NPM Packages For the Release Candidate, please update each Skeleton package to the early access `@next` version. Note that we will be updating these package frequently leading up to the full release. **React** ```bash npm install @skeletonlabs/skeleton@latest @skeletonlabs/skeleton-react@latest ``` **Svelte** ```bash npm install @skeletonlabs/skeleton@latest @skeletonlabs/skeleton-svelte@latest ``` ### Update Stylesheet Imports The following migration steps apply to your global stylesheet (ex: `app.css`). The `@source` rules for sourcing component styles have been replaced with simpler and more intuitive `@import` rules. ```css @source '../node_modules/@skeletonlabs/skeleton-{framework}/dist'; /* [!code --] */ @import '@skeletonlabs/skeleton-{framework}'; /* [!code ++] */ ``` In Skeleton v3 the [Preset styles](/docs/\[framework]/design/presets) were split to an optional stylesheet to allow them to be opt-in. However, due to popular demand, these have now been merged back into the core package. If this optional import is present, please remove it: ```css @import '@skeletonlabs/skeleton/optional/presets'; /* [!code --] */ ``` *** ## Manual Migration Assuming all above steps have been completed in full, you will now be required to handle the manual portion of the migration. The steps below are not optional and must be completed to finalize your migration to Skeleton v4. ### Migrating Components Below is an example of that process specifically tailored for the Avatar component. **Was (v3)** ```svelte ``` **Now (v4)** ```svelte SK ``` 1. Consult the documentation for the [Avatar component](/docs/\[framework]/framework-components/avatar) 2. Keep the import as is; no changes are required for this component. 3. The root component `` remains; remove the `src`, `alt`, and or `name` props. 4. Implement the new `` child and pass the `src` path and `alt` text. 5. Initials are no longer generated via the `name`; instead specify a fallback with `` Until your components are migrated to the new syntax and APIs they will cause errors when trying to run your local developement server. As such, we recommend using the following technique to hanlde this in a progressive manner: 1. Use your editor of choice to search for Skeleton components via the import path (`@skeletonlabs/skeleton`) 2. Comment out each component one-by-one until all are disabled. 3. Consider adding messages such as `

(avatar)

` as visible placeholders for components on each page. 4. Uncomment each component individually as you migrate it forward to the new syntax. 5. Once all components are migrated, you should no longer receive errors when running the dev server. ### Rename Components As part of this update, select components have been renamed. We now aim to follow the [Zag.js](https://zagjs.com/) naming conventions. \| Was (v3) | Now (v4) | v4 Docs | \| ------------------- | ---------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- | \| `` | `` | [View](/docs/\[framework]/framework-components/dialog) | \| `` | `` | [View](/docs/\[framework]/framework-components/navigation) | \| `` | `` | [View](/docs/\[framework]/framework-components/navigation) | \| `` | `` | [View](/docs/\[framework]/framework-components/progress-circular) | \| `` | `` | [View](/docs/\[framework]/framework-components/rating-group) | \| `` | `` | [View](/docs/\[framework]/framework-components/segmented-control) | \| `` | `` | [View](/docs/\[framework]/framework-components/toast) | *** ## Support and Feedback If you have any questions or issues about the migration process, please contact us on [Discord](https://discord.gg/EXqV7W8MtY) (the `#contributors` channel) or via [GitHub](https://github.com/skeletonlabs/skeleton/discussions). We are here to help!

tasks.title: Documentation process improvement
tasks.summary:
You are working on the Kwipoo project.

Context:
- There is the app itself and the website.
- Documentation for how to use the app, what features exist, what terms mean, what fields mean, and what different entities/objects contain needs to live on the website.
- The app is still changing fairly quickly, so documentation can become stale easily.
- We want a streamlined, low-friction process for keeping docs accurate as the app evolves and for getting updated documentation deployed to the website.
- There is already some versioning / release infrastructure in place, including versions and some Changesets/release-related setup.
- The app and website live under a shared local project folder.
- The goal is not just “write docs,” but to design and implement a practical system/process that makes docs easier to maintain going forward.

Your task:
Review the current project structure, release/versioning flow, and any existing documentation setup, then design and begin implementing a streamlined documentation maintenance and publishing workflow.

What I want you to do:
1. Inspect the codebase and determine:
   - where the app code lives
   - where the website code lives
   - where documentation currently lives, if anywhere
   - how the website currently consumes or renders documentation
   - what release/versioning/changelog mechanisms already exist
   - whether there is any current CI/CD path that could be extended for docs updates/deployment

2. Based on the current project, propose a practical documentation architecture that is:
   - simple
   - maintainable by a very small team
   - resilient to frequent app changes
   - low-overhead
   - not overengineered

3. Specifically solve for these needs:
   - documenting features, terms, entities, fields, and usage
   - making it easy to update docs when app features change
   - reducing the chance that docs drift out of sync with the app
   - making docs easy to publish to the website
   - fitting docs updates into the existing development/release workflow as naturally as possible

4. Think through whether docs should be:
   - stored in the website repo/app directly
   - stored as markdown/mdsvex/content files
   - partially generated from structured definitions or schemas where appropriate
   - tied to releases/changesets in some way
   - checked by CI for missing updates or stale references

5. Identify where documentation can realistically be sourced from or anchored to, such as:
   - route definitions
   - UI labels
   - forms and field definitions
   - entity/type definitions
   - schemas
   - config files
   - existing copy/content
   - changelogs or release notes

6. Recommend what should be manual vs semi-automated vs automated.
   Be pragmatic. Do not force automation where it adds fragility or complexity.

Deliverables:
- A concise assessment of the current documentation/update/deployment situation
- A proposed workflow for how documentation should be maintained going forward
- A recommended file/folder structure for docs
- A recommendation for how docs should be rendered on the website
- A recommendation for how docs updates should fit into the dev/release workflow
- Any recommended CI checks, scripts, or conventions
- A prioritized implementation plan

Implementation:
After the assessment and proposal, begin implementing the foundation of the recommended approach.
Focus on the minimum useful version first.

That likely means some combination of:
- creating a docs structure
- adding or improving a docs rendering pipeline on the website
- creating reusable docs templates or frontmatter conventions
- adding scripts/checks to support docs maintenance
- adding a lightweight process for tying feature changes to docs updates
- documenting the workflow for the team in a maintainer-facing doc

Important constraints:
- Keep this lightweight and pragmatic for a small fast-moving startup project.
- Avoid enterprise-style process bloat.
- Avoid creating a huge abstract documentation system unless it clearly pays off.
- Prefer explicit, understandable patterns over cleverness.
- Optimize for ease of upkeep, not theoretical elegance.
- Where possible, use what already exists in the project instead of inventing parallel systems.
- Flag any areas where the current codebase structure makes accurate docs harder than it should be.

What I do NOT want:
- a generic explanation of why documentation matters
- a giant speculative redesign of the whole app/site
- heavy process for its own sake
- docs that are disconnected from the actual product structure
- fake automation that looks smart but will be annoying to maintain

What I want instead:
- a grounded review of the current project
- concrete recommendations
- practical implementation
- a maintainable workflow that helps keep docs accurate and easy to publish

Output format:
1. Current-state findings
2. Risks / pain points
3. Recommended documentation architecture
4. Recommended maintenance + publishing workflow
5. Recommended implementation plan
6. Actual changes made
7. Follow-up recommendations

tasks.title: Reduce /things delta sync full-table scans and snapshot rebuilds
tasks.summary: Implement the next evidence-backed performance fix for Kwipoo by reducing the /things delta-refresh path that still behaves like a near-full refresh. Narrow the server and client work behind `since`-based collection refreshes so large inventories do not pay for full id scans, full snapshot reconstruction, and broad local rewrites on ordinary returning visits, and capture before/after evidence for route data work.
tasks.successCriteria: The /things delta-refresh path no longer performs unnecessary near-full-refresh work during ordinary `since`-based sync, existing thing/set/set-slot/set-item behavior still works, and the change includes before/after evidence showing lower query or merge cost.
tasks.readyCondition: The route-loading evidence task is complete and the `/places` and `/profile` follow-on route fixes have already been handled separately.
tasks.expectedOutcome: Returning users with existing local data hit a meaningfully cheaper /things refresh path while preserving correct inventory, set, and set-item behavior.

delegationPacket.objective: Apply the next core inventory performance fix by making `/things` delta sync behave like a true incremental refresh instead of a near-full collection rebuild.
delegationPacket.inputContext: Use the completed route-loading evidence and the finished `/places` and `/profile` route-data fixes. Focus on `thingsPageController`, `thingsCollectionsData`, `thingsSetItemsData`, delta-mode query shape, snapshot rebuild helpers, and the local merge path that rewrites broad user-scope data even during incremental refresh.
delegationPacket.expectedDeliverable: A patch that materially reduces unnecessary `/things` delta-sync work, plus a concise before/after evidence note for the route.
delegationPacket.doneCondition: The ordinary `/things` refresh path with existing local data no longer pays for avoidable full-table scans or full snapshot rebuild work unless a real full resync condition requires it, and the route still renders correct inventory, sets, and set items.
delegationPacket.integrationNotes: Keep the work scoped to `/things` delta-sync cost reduction. Do not fold in chat scaling, connectivity polling, or unrelated UI rendering cleanup unless they are strictly required for correctness.

tasks.title: Reduce authenticated shell background polling and listener churn
tasks.summary: Implement the next evidence-backed performance fix for Kwipoo by reducing always-on authenticated background work in the app shell. Remove or narrow the 1-second connectivity polling path, clean up long-lived shell listeners so they do not duplicate or leak across mounts, and capture before/after idle evidence for authenticated browser or wrapper sessions.
tasks.successCriteria: Authenticated sessions no longer pay for unnecessary 1-second connectivity polling or duplicate shell listeners during ordinary idle use, connectivity and push behavior still work correctly, and the change includes before/after evidence showing lower idle background work.
tasks.readyCondition: The background-scaling evidence task is complete and the route-specific startup and core data-loading fixes have already been handled separately.
tasks.expectedOutcome: Authenticated sessions stay quieter at idle, with lower wrapper battery and listener churn risk, while preserving correct connectivity and push setup behavior.

delegationPacket.objective: Apply the next cross-route performance fix by removing always-on shell churn that affects every authenticated session, especially wrapper users.
delegationPacket.inputContext: Use the completed background-scaling evidence and the finished startup-shell split work. Focus on `src/stores/connectivity.ts`, `src/lib/platform/rootShell.ts`, `src/lib/platform/appShellBootstrap.ts`, `src/pushNotifications.ts`, `src/firebase.ts`, and `src/index.ts`, especially the 1000 ms connectivity interval, missing cleanup paths, and duplicate or mismatched messaging listeners.
delegationPacket.expectedDeliverable: A patch that reduces authenticated idle background churn, plus a concise before/after evidence note for idle session behavior.
delegationPacket.doneCondition: The authenticated shell no longer relies on avoidable 1-second polling or leaked/duplicated listeners for ordinary idle operation unless a real platform constraint requires it, and connectivity plus push behavior still work correctly.
delegationPacket.integrationNotes: Keep the work scoped to connectivity polling and shell listener lifecycle cleanup. Do not fold in chat data-shape or collection-render optimizations unless they are strictly required for correctness.

tasks.title: Reduce /chats full-history scans and duplicate realtime work
tasks.summary: Implement the next evidence-backed performance fix for Kwipoo by making the `/chats` route scale with the visible conversation state instead of total message history. Narrow the initial message load and repeated derived scans, prevent avoidable duplicate realtime subscriptions when the list and active chat coexist, and capture before/after evidence on a chat-heavy account or seeded message set.
tasks.successCriteria: The `/chats` route no longer performs unnecessary full-history fetch or repeated whole-array scans for ordinary chat usage, active-chat realtime work is not duplicated without need, chat behavior still works correctly, and the change includes before/after evidence showing lower route or idle work on message-heavy data.
tasks.readyCondition: The background-scaling evidence task is complete and the authenticated shell background-churn follow-on has already been handled separately.
tasks.expectedOutcome: Chat-heavy users see a lighter `/chats` route that scales better with real message history while preserving correct chat list, open conversation, and realtime updates.

delegationPacket.objective: Apply the next paying-customer performance fix by reducing `/chats` work that currently grows with total message volume and duplicated realtime subscriptions.
delegationPacket.inputContext: Use the completed background-scaling evidence and the finished shell-background cleanup. Focus on `src/routes/(app)/chats/+page.server.ts`, `src/stores/messages.ts`, `src/stores/activeChat.ts`, `src/routes/(app)/chats/+page.svelte`, `src/routes/(app)/chats/ChatsPageDataSync.svelte`, and `src/routes/(app)/chats/ChatWindowSubscription.svelte`, especially the full-message SSR load, repeated derived scans, and overlapping chat-list plus chat-window subscriptions.
delegationPacket.expectedDeliverable: A patch that materially reduces `/chats` message-volume scaling cost, plus a concise before/after evidence note for chat-heavy usage.
delegationPacket.doneCondition: The ordinary `/chats` path no longer scales primarily with total historical message volume or duplicate active-source subscriptions unless a real product constraint requires it, and the chat list plus open conversation still behave correctly.
delegationPacket.integrationNotes: Keep the work scoped to `/chats` data-shape and realtime subscription cost. Do not fold in unrelated collection-render optimizations unless they are strictly required for correctness.

tasks.title: Reduce collection-heavy UI recompute on things, places, and notifications
tasks.summary: Implement the next evidence-backed performance fix for Kwipoo by reducing repeated whole-list and nested recompute work on populated inventory surfaces. Focus on the expensive derived filtering, grouping, per-row membership scans, place-list recomposition, and unread-notification processing that still make large accounts feel heavy, and capture before/after interaction evidence on representative data.
tasks.successCriteria: The targeted collection-heavy views no longer perform avoidable whole-array or nested recompute during ordinary interactions, things/sets/places/notifications behavior still works correctly, and the change includes before/after evidence showing lower render or interaction cost on larger data.
tasks.readyCondition: The background-scaling evidence task is complete and the shell-background plus chat-scaling follow-on tasks have already been handled separately.
tasks.expectedOutcome: Users with larger inventories or more notifications get faster list, detail, and filter interactions on the core authenticated surfaces without changing product behavior.

delegationPacket.objective: Apply the next responsiveness fix by reducing collection-heavy UI recompute on the main inventory and notifications surfaces.
delegationPacket.inputContext: Use the completed background-scaling evidence and the finished `/things` sync plus `/chats` follow-ons. Focus on `src/routes/(app)/things/ThingList.svelte`, `src/routes/(app)/things/SetList.svelte`, `src/routes/(app)/things/ThingDetail.svelte`, `src/routes/(app)/places/+page.svelte`, `src/features/notifications/store.ts`, and `src/routes/(app)/profile/notifications/Notifications.svelte`, especially repeated full-array filters, nested `some` checks, per-row scans, and list rebuilds on state changes.
delegationPacket.expectedDeliverable: A patch that reduces interaction-time recompute on collection-heavy screens, plus a concise before/after evidence note for representative large-data interactions.
delegationPacket.doneCondition: The targeted list and detail surfaces no longer rely on avoidable repeated whole-list recompute for ordinary user interactions unless a real product constraint requires it, and the affected inventory plus notification views still render correct content.
delegationPacket.integrationNotes: Keep the work scoped to collection-heavy render and interaction cost. Do not fold in unrelated server query or shell-lifecycle work unless it is strictly required for correctness.

tasks.title: Rebaseline Kwipoo performance and decide residual follow-up
tasks.summary: Run a final performance verification pass across the main authenticated flows after the completed startup, route-data, background, chat, and collection-render fixes. Re-measure the key routes, compare against the earlier evidence notes, capture the strongest remaining bottlenecks if any, and decide whether the parent performance effort can close or whether one final targeted follow-up is still justified.
tasks.successCriteria: A concise evidence note compares the current state against the earlier Kwipoo baselines, confirms the major fixes held up in an integrated build, and clearly states either that the parent task is ready to close or what single residual issue still deserves another execution task.
tasks.readyCondition: The completed performance implementation tasks for startup, `/places`, `/profile`, `/things`, shell background churn, `/chats`, and collection-heavy recompute are all available for integrated verification.
tasks.expectedOutcome: The parent performance effort has an up-to-date end-state measurement and a defensible decision on whether any meaningful bottleneck remains before closing the work.

delegationPacket.objective: Verify the cumulative effect of the completed Kwipoo performance fixes and identify whether any remaining bottleneck is important enough to warrant one last follow-up task.
delegationPacket.inputContext: Use the existing evidence artifacts, including startup baseline and after files, route-loading evidence, background-scaling evidence, and the implementation evidence notes for `/places`, `/things`, `/chats`, and collection-heavy recompute. Re-measure the main authenticated routes and compare against the earlier findings instead of starting from scratch.
delegationPacket.expectedDeliverable: A final markdown evidence note with updated measurements, comparison against prior baselines, residual risks, and a recommendation to either close the parent task or queue one last narrowly scoped follow-up.
delegationPacket.doneCondition: The cumulative performance changes have been re-measured, the remaining risks are ranked by real user impact, and the parent task has a clear closure recommendation grounded in current evidence.
delegationPacket.integrationNotes: Do not expand this into another broad optimization pass. Keep the work focused on integrated verification, residual-risk ranking, and closure guidance.

tasks.title: Finish /profile first-mount hydration and remove duplicate collections refresh
tasks.summary: Implement the final performance follow-up identified by the integrated rebaseline: make `/profile` trust the already-complete SSR collections payload on first mount so it does not immediately re-fetch `/api/profile/collections` and re-run avoidable hydration work. Keep the route behavior correct, capture before/after route timings, and treat this as the last targeted performance task before closing the parent effort.
tasks.successCriteria: The `/profile` route no longer issues an unnecessary immediate `/api/profile/collections` refresh after first paint when the SSR payload is already complete, profile tabs and counts still work correctly, and the change includes before/after evidence showing materially better profile route timing.
tasks.readyCondition: The final rebaseline task is complete and its evidence confirms `/profile` is the only remaining meaningful user-facing performance bottleneck.
tasks.expectedOutcome: The only clearly heavy authenticated route is brought in line with the rest of the app, leaving the parent performance effort ready to close if the targeted verification passes.

delegationPacket.objective: Apply the one residual performance fix that still matters after the integrated rebaseline by removing `/profile`'s duplicate first-mount collections refresh and excess hydration cost.
delegationPacket.inputContext: Use `kwipoo-performance-rebaseline-evidence.md` plus the earlier route-loading evidence and prior `/profile` follow-up attempt. Focus on `src/routes/(app)/profile/+page.server.ts`, `src/routes/(app)/profile/profilePageController.ts`, and `src/lib/platform/pageDataBootstrap.ts`, especially the path that still calls `/api/profile/collections` after SSR has already loaded full collections.
delegationPacket.expectedDeliverable: A patch that prevents the unnecessary initial `/profile` collection refresh and a concise before/after evidence note proving the route is no longer the outlier.
delegationPacket.doneCondition: The ordinary `/profile` first load trusts the SSR collection payload instead of immediately duplicating it, the route still renders correct profile summaries and tabs, and updated measurements show that `/profile` is no longer the clear remaining bottleneck.
delegationPacket.integrationNotes: Keep this as the last narrowly scoped performance fix. Do not reopen broader startup, chat, or collection-render work unless it is strictly required for correctness.

tasks.title: Reduce /profile SSR payload and above-the-fold hydration cost
tasks.summary: Implement the last evidence-backed profile performance fix by reducing the oversized `/profile` SSR document payload and the above-the-fold hydration work it drives. Keep profile counts and primary summary surfaces correct, avoid shipping the full owned collections payload in the first document when it is not needed immediately, and capture before/after route evidence proving `/profile` is no longer the clear outlier.
tasks.successCriteria: The `/profile` route ships materially less SSR document payload or first-hydration work on ordinary loads, profile summary and tab behavior still work correctly, and updated measurements show `/profile` is no longer dramatically slower than the other main authenticated routes.
tasks.readyCondition: The duplicate first-mount `/api/profile/collections` refresh has been removed, and the latest evidence shows the remaining `/profile` cost is now dominated by the SSR document payload rather than the removed refresh.
tasks.expectedOutcome: The last clearly heavy authenticated route is reduced to a level that no longer stands apart from `/places`, `/things`, and `/chats`, leaving the parent performance effort ready to close.

delegationPacket.objective: Apply the final residual performance fix by shrinking `/profile`'s first-document payload and above-the-fold hydration cost now that the duplicate collections refresh is gone.
delegationPacket.inputContext: Use `kwipoo-profile-first-mount-fix-evidence.md`, `kwipoo-profile-final-verification.json`, and `kwipoo-performance-rebaseline-evidence.md`. Focus on `src/routes/(app)/profile/+page.server.ts`, `src/lib/server/profileCollectionsData.ts`, and the profile route's initial data contract, especially the warm `decodedBodySize` around `3.27 MB` and the fact that `/profile` remains materially slower even without the duplicate refresh.
delegationPacket.expectedDeliverable: A patch that materially reduces `/profile` first-load payload or hydration cost, plus a concise before/after evidence note showing the route is no longer the obvious outlier.
delegationPacket.doneCondition: Ordinary `/profile` loads no longer ship or hydrate avoidable collection-heavy SSR payload for above-the-fold content unless a real product constraint requires it, the route still renders correct summary and collection-tab behavior, and updated verification shows the remaining gap to other main routes is no longer dramatic.
delegationPacket.integrationNotes: Keep this scoped to `/profile` first-load payload and hydration cost. Do not reopen broad app-wide performance work unless it is strictly required for correctness.

tasks.title: Capture durable learnings from recent work

tasks.title: Investigate and fix the pre-existing things-offline Playwright failure
tasks.summary:
# Objective
Investigate the existing failure in `tests/e2e/things-offline.spec.js` that happens before the recently touched secondary-context path is exercised.

## Context
- The current failure occurs in `createThing(...)` and/or `expectEntityHeadingVisible(...)`.
- This is blocking confidence in the broader offline and regression coverage around the recent transfer/onboarding work.
- Treat this as a pre-existing failure unless the investigation proves otherwise.

## Expected Work
- Reproduce the failure reliably.
- Identify the root cause.
- Implement the narrowest credible fix if the issue is in the product or test harness.
- If the failure is outside the intended scope or should not be fixed yet, document exactly why and leave the suite in a clearly understood state.

## Deliverable
A fix or a precise root-cause report with evidence, plus the verification commands and outcome.
tasks.successCriteria: The failing things-offline spec is either fixed and passing, or the root cause and blocker are documented with enough evidence for a follow-up handoff.
tasks.readyCondition: The Kwipoo repo is available locally and the failing Playwright spec can be run in the preview test environment.
tasks.expectedOutcome: The team can explain why the offline spec was failing and can either rely on it again or defer it intentionally with a clear documented reason.

tasks.title: Run a broader Kwipoo preview regression pass after the transfer/onboarding fixes
tasks.summary:
# Objective
Run a broader preview-environment regression pass in the Kwipoo app after the recent transfer/onboarding fixes.

## Context
- The targeted onboarding and transfer checks have already passed.
- We still want a wider confidence check beyond those focused validations.

## Expected Work
- Choose a broader but still pragmatic preview regression set.
- Run the selected preview Playwright coverage.
- Capture failures, flakes, and noteworthy warnings.
- Summarize what passed, what failed, and what follow-up work is still needed.

## Deliverable
A concise regression report with commands run, pass/fail status, and any newly discovered issues.
tasks.successCriteria: A broader preview regression set has been executed and summarized, with any new failures or flakes called out clearly.
tasks.readyCondition: The preview test environment is runnable and the blocker from the things-offline failure has been investigated enough to know whether it should be included or excluded.
tasks.expectedOutcome: The recent transfer/onboarding changes have a wider validation signal than the targeted checks alone.

tasks.title: Confirm release-note coverage for the transfer and onboarding fixes
tasks.summary:
# Objective
Confirm whether the recent transfer and onboarding fixes and any other uncommitted need a Changeset or other release-note coverage in the Kwipoo repo.

## Context
- The code changes are complete enough to validate, but release metadata may still be missing.
- This task is about shipping hygiene, not additional product behavior changes.

## Expected Work
- Inspect the repo release process and recent changes.
- Determine whether a Changeset is required for the transfer/onboarding fixes.
- Add the Changeset if needed, or document why one is not needed.

## Deliverable
A clear yes/no decision on release-note coverage, with the supporting repo evidence and any required artifact added.
tasks.successCriteria: There is an explicit answer on whether release-note coverage is required, and the repo either contains the required Changeset or a documented justification for not adding one.
tasks.readyCondition: The recent transfer/onboarding change set is identifiable in the Kwipoo repo and the project release process can be inspected.
tasks.expectedOutcome: The transfer/onboarding fixes have the right release metadata before they ship.

tasks.title: Make SQLite the only writable runtime store and add drift protection
tasks.summary:
# Objective
Eliminate runtime drift between `data/app.sqlite` and `data/control-plane.json` so the control-plane store has one clear source of truth.

## Context
- The app runtime reads from SQLite, while JSON still exists for export/import and initial seeding.
- Recent role creation work exposed that editing or syncing only one side can leave the UI and file snapshot disagreeing.
- This is a data-integrity and operator-experience issue, not just a one-off role bug.

## Expected Work
- Make SQLite the only normal writable runtime store for control-plane data.
- Turn the JSON files into explicit export/import artifacts rather than live runtime mirrors.
- Add drift detection or a loud warning/failure path in development when SQLite and JSON diverge.
- Make the supported mutation path clear in code and docs so agents and operators do not patch JSON directly.
- Add a regression check or test covering creation/update flows through the real runtime store.

## Deliverable
A repo change that removes or sharply reduces SQLite/JSON drift risk, with docs and validation that make the storage contract explicit.
tasks.successCriteria: The control-plane runtime has one clearly writable source of truth, JSON is treated as export/import-only or otherwise protected from silent drift, divergence is detected loudly, and the supported mutation path is documented and regression-covered.
tasks.readyCondition: The current control-plane storage flow, JSON mirror behavior, and task/role mutation paths can be inspected in the repo.
tasks.expectedOutcome: Control-plane mutations go through one trusted runtime path and storage drift becomes either impossible or immediately obvious.

decisions.summary: Feature draft and design artifact is ready. It defines the MVP slice, shell-level assistant UX, context/action-plan contracts, implementation sequence, and the blocking product decision around what an agent record should mean.

Expanded the AI branch with durable-agent runtime notes, including durable execution, session state, checkpointing, recovery, and idempotency, and wired them into the taxonomy plus the existing agent-runtime, observability, and management-system notes.
Approved the thread response and completed the task from the thread detail page.

Requirements gathering artifact is ready for review. It clarifies operator need, scope boundaries, current-system constraints, proposed V1 success signal, and the unresolved agent-model decision that should be settled before implementation.
Approved the thread response and completed the task from the thread detail page.

Expanded the AI branch with agent-runtime evaluation metrics, including task success rate, intervention rate, escalation rate, time to resolution, and cost to success, and wired them into the taxonomy plus the existing observability, evaluation, and agent-system notes.

Expanded the AI branch with ranked-retrieval evaluation notes, including precision at k, recall at k, mean reciprocal rank, discounted cumulative gain, and normalized discounted cumulative gain, and wired them into the taxonomy plus the existing evaluation and RAG-evaluation notes.

Expanded the AI branch with retrieval-optimization notes, including BM25, hybrid search, query rewriting, reciprocal rank fusion, and cross-encoders, and wired them into the taxonomy plus the existing RAG, semantic-search, reranking, and overview notes.

Expanded the AI branch with agent-memory architecture notes, including working memory, episodic memory, semantic memory, artifact memory, and context compaction, and wired them into the taxonomy plus the existing agent, memory, context-engineering, and runtime notes.
Approved the thread response and completed the task from the thread detail page.

Aligned local and remote develop and colin-work to main at 40d8d30, fast-forwarded local main to origin/main, and created local backup tags branch-sync-backup/develop-20260422-2338 and branch-sync-backup/colin-work-20260422-2338.

Expanded the AI branch with serving-efficiency and decoding notes, including latency, throughput, batching, speculative decoding, beam search, and nucleus sampling, and wired them into the taxonomy plus the existing serving, inference, LLM, autoregressive-model, and overview notes.
Recovered thread thread_050a1837-d618-40c3-bc17-d3269e068c72 from the thread detail page and re-queued the latest request for Expand knowledge and understanding of ontologies and related topics.

Expanded the AI branch with practical retrieval and tool-use notes, including semantic search, chunking, reranking, approximate nearest neighbor search, tool calling, and function calling, and wired them into the taxonomy plus the existing RAG, vector database, agent, MCP, and overview notes.
Recovered thread thread_dcf385fc-64a6-4d82-92f1-af7c6c05dad6 from the thread detail page and re-queued the latest request for Research and expand knowledge on Artificial Intelligence.

Expanded the AI branch with runtime-serving and reliability notes, including benchmarking, calibration, robustness, adversarial examples, tokenization, context windows, KV cache, and model serving, and wired them into the existing evaluation, hallucination, inference, and taxonomy notes.

Created a lightweight file-based content OS workspace with core docs, templates, shared channel profiles, Kwipoo starter content units and outputs, inbox capture examples, and an AdventureWare scaffold.

Expanded the AI branch with core training mechanics and efficient adaptation notes, including gradient descent, backpropagation, optimizer and learning-rate concepts, plus PEFT and LoRA, and wired them into the existing LLM, loss, objective, fine-tuning, and inference notes.

Expanded the AI branch with post-training alignment methods, model compression and sparse-architecture notes, and a stronger safety/evaluation layer covering interpretability, red teaming, prompt injection, and links to existing AI governance material.

Expanded the AI branch again with classical supervised model families, sequential decision and control formalisms, robotics/control coverage, and a dedicated GraphRAG note tied back to the taxonomy and ML/RL hub notes.

Expanded the AI taxonomy further with generative-model subtypes, BERT-style versus GPT-style language-model distinctions, graph neural networks, and a speech/audio branch, while keeping model families separate from supporting infrastructure.
Expanded the AI branch with a new taxonomy note, stronger hub/model notes, and new notes that distinguish paradigms, model categories, model families, and AI-adjacent graph infrastructure.
